1: חָכְמוֹת (ḥokmôt) is a plural form — often understood as a plural of majesty or intensity, "Lady Wisdom" writ large, the same plural used in 1:20 and 9:1 where Wisdom is personified. The wise woman (ʾēšet ḥāyil implied) builds (bānātâ, perfect: accomplished action) her house; the fool tears it down with her own hands (bəyādehā) — the instrument of destruction is herself.
2: יְרֵא יהוה (fearer of the LORD) — the fear-of-YHWH formula; yārēʾ as a participle indicates a settled character, not a momentary fright. The contrast is bôzeh dərākāyw (one who despises his [the LORD's] ways) — the suffix "his ways" refers to the LORD's ways, not the person's own; contempt for God's path is contempt for God himself.
3: בְּפִי-אֱוִיל (in the mouth of a fool) is a חֹטֶר גַּאֲוָה (branch/shoot of pride/arrogance) — ḥōṭer is a twig or rod; the fool's speech is itself a weapon of arrogance that strikes back against him. The lips of the wise (śiptê ḥăkāmîm) tišmôrēm (protect them, Qal imperfect) — the lips are both the instrument and the means of protection.
4: אֵין אֲלָפִים (where there are no oxen) the אֵבוּס בָּר (manger is empty/clean) — the word bār can mean both "clean" and "grain/food." The deliberate ambiguity: clean because empty, or empty of the grain that oxen produce. Much increase (tebûʾat ʿāz, produce of strength) comes from ox power. Productivity requires accepting the mess of living things.
5: עֵד אֱמוּנִים (faithful/trustworthy witness) — ʾĕmûnîm carries the covenant-faithfulness register of ʾemet (truth, reliability); the witness is truthful because they are fundamentally oriented toward reality. The false witness (ʾēd šeqer, a witness of šeqer, falsehood/deceit) breathes out (yāpîaḥ) lies — the breathing metaphor suggests effortless, habitual production of deception.
6: The mocker (lēṣ) seeks (biqqēš, Piel: deliberate seeking) wisdom but does not find it. Knowledge comes easily (nāqāl, is light/swift) to the person of understanding (nābôn). The lēṣ's problem is not intellectual incapacity but the prior moral stance — contempt for correction (see Proverbs 13:1) that closes the mind to wisdom before the seeking begins.
7: לֵךְ מִנֶּגֶד (go from before) is a strong spatial imperative: physically remove yourself from the fool's presence. The reason: בַּל-יָדַעְתָּ שִׂפְתֵי-דָעַת — there are no lips of knowledge on him; knowledge is not present to his mouth. The negative bal (never/certainly not) is stronger than simple lōʾ.
8: עָרְמַת הַפִּקֵחַ (cleverness/prudence of the discerning) is to understand his way — the same root ʿāram used for the serpent's cleverness in Genesis 3:1; context determines whether shrewdness is virtue or vice. The folly of fools is mirmâ (deception/deceit) — the MKT "self-deception" draws out the direction: fools deceive themselves first.
9: אֱוִילִים יָלִיץ אָשָׁם — fools mock or scoff at the ʾāšām (guilt/guilt-offering). The word ʾāšām carries its full Levitical weight: the guilt offering was the mechanism for repairing a damaged relationship with God or others. Fools treat the concept of restitution/guilt as something to ridicule. Among the upright (yəšārîm), rāṣôn (favor/goodwill) is present.
10: מַרַּת נַפְשׁוֹ (bitterness of his soul) — mar is the taste of gall, the physical sensation of grief. Nephesh here is the inner experiential self, inaccessible to outsiders. No stranger (zār) shares in its joy either — the same inaccessibility applies to gladness. The verse establishes the fundamental privacy of the inner life as an anthropological fact.
11: בֵּית רְשָׁעִים (house of the wicked) will be destroyed (yiššāmēd, Niphal: will be caused to perish) — the house as a material symbol of the family line. The tent (ʾōhel) of the upright will flourish (yiphrāḥ). The pairing of "house" with "tent" is deliberate: even the more temporary structure of the upright outlasts the supposedly permanent house of the wicked.
12: דֶּרֶךְ יָשָׁר לִפְנֵי-אִישׁ (a way straight before a man) — the initial assessment is positive, "straight/upright" by appearance. Its end (ʾaḥărîteyhā) is ways of death (darkê-māvet, plural: there are many such paths). The verse is a philosophical statement about the limits of self-evaluation and the danger of trust in one's own moral sense (cf. Proverbs 3:5–7).
13: גַּם בִּשְׂחוֹק (even in laughter) the heart may be painful (yikʾāb, is in pain/hurt) — the imperfect here is present-tense observation: laughter does not guarantee inner peace. וְאַחֲרִיתָהּ שִׂמְחָה תוּגָה (and its end, joy is grief) — the feminine suffix on "its end" refers back to laughter; the grammatical trace is subtle. Joy contains the seed of its own reversal.
14: מִדְּרָכָיו יִשְׂבַּע (from his own ways he will be satisfied/full) — the backslider (sûg lēb, one who turns away of heart) is sated with his own choices: the consequences he chose. The good man will be satisfied mimmennû (from himself, from within) — the prep min makes the source internal, suggesting genuine inner resource rather than external circumstance.
15: פֶּתִי (petî, simple/naive one) — the naivety of inexperience, not malice; the term is used for the educable youth throughout Proverbs. Yaʾamin (believes) every word — the imperfect indicates habitual credulity. The prudent (ʿārûm) considers carefully (yābîn ʾăšurô, understands/discerns his step) — the verb bîn implies ongoing deliberation.
16: חָכָם יָרֵא (a wise person fears) — the object is implied; the fear is of God and consequence. He turns from evil (sār mērāʿ). The fool (kəsîl) is mitrabbēr (overconfident, puffed up) and bôṭēaḥ (trusts/is secure) — the same root used for godly trust in God; the fool redirects that trust toward himself.
17: קְצַר-אַפַּיִם (short of nostrils/anger, quick-tempered) — the Hebrew idiom for anger runs through the nostrils; ʾapayyim literally means nostrils, and a flaring nose signals fury. Verse 29 below pairs this with the positive: ʾerek ʾapayyim (long of nostrils, slow to anger). The anatomical metaphor is precise and visceral.
18: נָחֲלוּ פְתָאיִם (the naive have inherited) folly (ʾiwwelet) — the verb nāḥal is the inheritance-word; folly becomes their estate, their portion. The prudent (ʿărûmîm) are crowned (yaktiru, Hiphil: will crown/encircle) with knowledge. The social and metaphorical resonance of keter (crown) elevates knowledge to royalty.
19: שַׁחוּ (they bowed down, Qal perfect) — the prophetic perfect: the moral order demands this outcome so certainly that it is spoken as accomplished fact. Evil people bow before the good; the wicked kneel at the gates of the righteous. The gate (šaʿar) is the place of judgment and community life; the reversal happens precisely where honor is publicly distributed.
20: גַּם לְרֵעֵהוּ (even by his neighbor) is hated (yiśśānēʾ) — the social isolation of poverty. The rich man (ʿāšîr) has many friends (ʾōhăbîm rabbîm, many lovers) — the word ʾōhăbîm (from ʾāhab, love) in a social context means those who are drawn to him; the same root used for genuine love here denotes transactional attachment to wealth.
21: בָּז לְרֵעֵהוּ (despises his neighbor) חוֹטֵא (sins, is a sinner) — the participial form makes contempt for the neighbor a defining sin, not a minor failing. The one who shows grace (mēḥônnēn, Piel participle: continually shows grace) to the poor (ʿănāyîm) is called ʾašrāyw (blessed, happy) — the same beatitude form as Psalms 1:1.
22: חֹרְשֵׁי רָע (those who plow evil) — the agricultural metaphor: deliberate, sustained evil-cultivation. They yitʿû (go astray, wander) — the same verb used for straying sheep. Those who plan good (ḥōrəšê ṭôb) find ḥesed wĕʾemet (loyal love and faithfulness) — the covenant-pair that characterizes God's own character (Exodus 34:6).
23: בְּכָל-עֶצֶב (in all labor/painful toil) there is profit (môtār, something left over, surplus) — ʿeṣeb is the word used for the painful labor of Genesis 3:17 (the curse); here it is the path to gain. Debar śəpātayim (word of lips) — mere talking — leads only to poverty. The contrast is between embodied action and verbal substitutes for it.
24: עֲטֶרֶת חֲכָמִים עָשְׁרָם (the crown of the wise is their wealth) — ʿōšer (wealth) as the crowning achievement; the word carries both material and honorific weight. The folly of fools (ʾiwwelet kəsîlîm) is ʾiwwelet (folly) — the tautology is deliberate and damning: fools' crown is simply their own foolishness, which they display unwittingly.
25: עֵד אֱמֶת (witness of truth) מַצִּיל נְפָשׁוֹת (saves lives/souls) — māṣal in the Hiphil is a rescue/deliverance verb, used for God's deliverance in the Psalms. The false witness (ʾēd šeqer) breathes out (yāpîaḥ) lies (cf. Proverbs 14:5) — the same breathing metaphor, the habitual effortlessness of deception.
26: בְּיִרְאַת יהוה (in the fear of the LORD) is strong confidence (mibtaḥ ʿōz, a strong refuge/trust). The suffix ûbānāyw (and for his children) — God's covenant with the God-fearer extends to the next generation; the fear of the LORD as an inherited orientation and social structure.
27: This verse is nearly identical to Proverbs 13:14: יְרֵאַת יהוה מְקוֹר חַיִּים (fear of the LORD is a fountain of life). The repetition is deliberate and structural; the wisdom collection reinforces its core affirmations by recurrence. The fountain of life is not an abstraction but a relational orientation toward God that produces vitality.
28: בְּרָב-עָם (in the abundance/multitude of people) is a king's glory (hadar) — the same word used for God's splendor in the Psalms. The prince's ruin (mahattâ, downfall) is the lack of people (bĕʾepes leʾōm, in the absence of a nation) — power is measured in persons, not territory. Leadership is relational.
29: אֶרֶךְ אַפַּיִם (ʾerek ʾapayyim) — literally "long of nostrils," the anatomical idiom for patience/slowness to anger, contrasting with qəṣar-ʾapayyim (short of nostrils, Proverbs 14:17). The contrast shows great insight (rab-tebûnâ, abundant understanding) — tebûnâ is not raw intelligence but the discernment that comes from understanding relationships and consequences. A quick temper (qəṣar-rûaḥ, short of spirit) elevates foolishness: makes it prominent, lifts it up.
30: לֵב מַרְפֵּא (a healing heart) is ḥayyê bāśār (life of flesh/the body) — the inner emotional state of the heart (lēb) generates the physical vitality of the whole body. Qinʾâ (envy/jealousy) rots the bones (raqqab-ʿăṣāmôt) — the same verb used for decay and putrefaction; the emotion produces biological deterioration. The psychosomatic connection is explicit.
31: עֹשֵׁק דָּל (oppressor of the poor) insults (ḥērēp) his Maker — the same word used for the Philistine's taunting of Israel (Goliath). To oppress the poor is to mock God as Creator. The one who shows grace (mēḥannēn) to the needy honors him (məkabbədhô) — the Piel of kābad, to give weight, to honor.
32: בְּרָעָתוֹ יִדָּחֶה רָשָׁע (in his evil the wicked is thrust down/driven away) — the passive Niphal: his own evil is the agent. חֹסֶה בְמוֹתוֹ צַדִּיק (the righteous has refuge in his death) — ḥāsâ (take refuge) is the verb of shelter and trust used throughout the Psalms. Bəmôtô (in his death) is striking: the righteous person's hope in the moment of death itself.
33: בְּלֶב נָבוֹן תָּנוּחַ חָכְמָה (in the heart of the discerning wisdom rests quietly) — tānûaḥ (rests, settles) is the verb of the Sabbath rest; wisdom finds a home in the discerning heart. Among fools (bəqereb kəsîlîm), wisdom "makes itself known" (tiggāḏaʿ, Niphal: is made known, reveals itself) — even unintentional disclosure; wisdom cannot be entirely suppressed even in fool company.
34: צְדָקָה תְּרוֹמֵם-גּוֹי (righteousness lifts up a nation) — the verb rûm in the Polel is a stately elevation; ṣedāqâ as the national character creates social height. Ḥesed (in many translations "reproach"; others "loyal love/steadfast love") — here ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/sin-offering) is the subject: "sin is the shame of peoples." The MKT "sin brings disgrace" follows the more common reading.
35: רְצוֹן-מֶלֶךְ (favor of the king) — rāṣôn is the same word as divine acceptance or favor; royal and divine approval run in parallel in wisdom literature. The servant who acts wisely (meskîl, Hiphil participle: causes insight/acts intelligently) receives this. The king's wrath (ʾapô, his nose/anger) falls on the shameful (mēbîš, one who causes shame).
